title: Meyniel's Conjecture (Conjecture 1.1)
statement_text: Let $G$ be a connected graph on $n$ vertices. Then $c(G) = O(\sqrt{n})$.
context_text: Described as perhaps the most well-known conjecture in the cops-and-robber area. Despite much interest, the best known general upper bound remains $n^{2-(1+o(1))\sqrt{\log n}}$, far from the conjectured $O(\sqrt{n})$. The paper proves an analogue for random $k$-uniform hypergraphs and introduces Conjecture 1.4 as a direct generalisation.
paper_contribution: Proves that whp the cop number of the $k$-uniform binomial random hypergraph $G^k(n,p)$ is $\tilde{O}\!\left(\sqrt{n/k}\right)$ for a broad range of $k$ and $p$ (Corollary 1.8), establishing the hypergraph analogue of Meyniel's conjecture in the random setting.
notes: PDF source — math notation may be garbled; $O(\sqrt{n})$ bound reconstructed from abstract and standard literature.
paper_title: Catching a robber on a random $k$-uniform hypergraph

title: Conjecture 1.4
statement_text: Let $H$ be a connected $k$-graph on $n$ vertices. Then $c(H) = O\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{n}{k}}\right)$.
context_text: The authors introduce this as a generalisation of Meyniel's conjecture to $k$-uniform hypergraphs, motivated by a blow-up construction that shows $c(H) = \Omega\!\left(\sqrt{n/k}\right)$ is achievable. They further observe that for any polynomial $k = n^\alpha$ with $\alpha < 1$, Conjecture 1.4 implies Meyniel's conjecture.
notes: PDF source — LaTeX reconstructed from abstract statement and the formal conjecture environment; statement is unambiguous from context.
paper_title: Catching a robber on a random $k$-uniform hypergraph
